name: Valdemoro and Torrejón de Velasco Castle Loop
name_original: Castillo de Torrejón de Velasco desde Valdemoro – circular por los Cerros Pelango y Portillo
description: Cycle a moderate 24.5-mile route from Valdemoro, exploring Cerros Pelango and Portillo, and the historic Castillo de Torrejón de Velasco.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Valdemoro offers diverse landscapes, including plains, rolling hills, and river valleys. The region features areas like the Cerros Pelango and Portillo, and the fluvial landscape surrounding the Jarama River. Routes often extend into the Southeast Regional Park, a protected area with rich flora and fauna. The dramatic Jarama gypsum cliffs also add a unique geological element to the scenery. Best touring cycling routes around Valdemoro The most popular touring cycling route is Valdemoro and Torrejón de Velasco Castle Loop, a 24.5 miles (39.4 km) trail that takes 3 hours 3 minutes to complete. This moderate route explores the local landscape and passes by the historic Torrejón de Velasco Castle. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Guatén Stream Crossing – View of the Countryside loop from Valdemoro, a moderate 28.7 miles (46.1 km) path. It offers scenic views of the countryside and crosses the Arroyo Guatén. Local touring cyclists also love the Cerro El Portillo – Cerro del Portillo loop from Valdemoro, a 15.1 miles (24.3 km) trail leading through the Cerro El Portillo hills, often completed in about 2 hours 15 minutes. Touring cycling around Valdemoro is defined by plains, rolling hills, and river valleys, with routes extending into the Southeast Regional Park. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate circular routes to more challenging trails. The routes in Valdemoro are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 40 reviews. More than 900 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Valdemoro's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Valdemoro?
A: There are over 70 touring cycling routes available around Valdemoro, catering to various skill levels. This includes 8 easy routes, 49 moderate routes, and 14 more challenging options for experienced cyclists.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Valdemoro?
A: Valdemoro offers a diverse cycling landscape, featuring plains, rolling hills, and river valleys. You'll find a mix of paved roads and unpaved tracks. Notable natural features include the Cerros Pelango and Portillo, the fluvial landscape around the Jarama River, and the dramatic Jarama gypsum cliffs.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Valdemoro?
A: Yes, Valdemoro has several routes suitable for beginners and families. For an accessible option, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6922813" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parque del Egido de la Fuente – Guatén Stream Crossing loop from Valdemoro&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 17.7-mile (28.5 km) path with relatively gentle elevation changes.

Q: What are some challenging touring cycling routes for experienced cyclists in Valdemoro?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, Valdemoro offers routes with more significant elevation gains and unpaved sections. While specific 'difficult' routes are not detailed here, the region's diverse terrain, including rolling hills and river valleys, provides ample opportunity for longer and more demanding rides.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Valdemoro?
A: Many of the touring cycling routes around Valdemoro are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e959790978/valdemoro-and-torrejon-de-velasco-castle-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valdemoro and Torrejón de Velasco Castle Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a 24.5-mile (39.4 km) moderate route.

Q: What natural features or viewpoints can I see on touring cycling routes near Valdemoro?
A: Cycling routes often lead through picturesque areas like the Cerros Pelango and Portillo, and along the Jarama River. You can also explore sections of the Southeast Regional Park, known for its rich flora and fauna. For specific viewpoints, consider routes that offer glimpses of the dramatic Jarama gypsum cliffs or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2667434" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Birdwatching Hide at San Juan Lagoons&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there historical landmarks or points of interest along the touring cycling routes?
A: Yes, several routes incorporate historical landmarks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e959790978/valdemoro-and-torrejon-de-velasco-castle-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valdemoro and Torrejón de Velasco Castle Loop&lt;/a&gt; passes by the historic Castillo de Torrejón de Velasco. You might also spot the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5291395" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puñoenrostro Castle&lt;/a&gt; from certain paths, or pass by the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1393652" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Teja Spring and the Hermitage of San Isidro&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the typical distance and elevation gain for touring cycling routes in Valdemoro?
A: Routes vary significantly, but many popular touring cycling paths in Valdemoro are moderate in length and elevation. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6911786" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guatén Stream Crossing – View of the Countryside loop from Valdemoro&lt;/a&gt; covers 28.7 miles (46.1 km) with an elevation gain of approximately 288 meters. Shorter routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6922415" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro El Portillo – Cerro del Portillo loop from Valdemoro&lt;/a&gt; are around 15.1 miles (24.3 km) with about 210 meters of ascent.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy the most about touring cycling in Valdemoro?
A: The touring cycling routes in Valdemoro are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 40 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse landscapes, from plains to rolling hills and river valleys, as well as the opportunity to explore natural areas like the Southeast Regional Park and historical sites.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of the countryside?
A: Absolutely. Many routes provide scenic views of the surrounding countryside. A great option for this is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6911786" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guatén Stream Crossing – View of the Countryside loop from Valdemoro&lt;/a&gt;, which offers expansive vistas and crosses the picturesque Arroyo Guatén.

Q: Can I find routes that connect Valdemoro to other towns?
A: Valdemoro's strategic location makes it a convenient starting point for longer cycling excursions to nearby towns. While specific routes connecting directly to other towns are not listed here, the region is part of the CiclaMadrid network, which includes stages linking Valdemoro to places like Aranjuez, a World Heritage City known for its royal palace and gardens.

This is a good place to stop and look around. You will see how flat and dry this area is between Pinto and Valdemoro.

Impressive castle built in the fifteenth century. It has a towered perimeter, where the homage tower stands out, at the base of which is the access to the interior of the castle. It was in a state of ruin until a two-phase restoration was carried out that ended in 2018.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: This circular touring bicycle route is designed to be completed in approximately 3 hours and 3 minutes of riding time. However, factoring in stops for sightseeing, breaks, and navigating varied terrain, you should plan for a total duration of up to 5 hours.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: The route is classified as moderate and requires good fitness due to its varied surfaces and some potentially challenging segments. While not for absolute beginners, cyclists with some experience and good physical condition will find it an engaging ride. Some sections are explicitly mentioned as difficult to ride.

faqs.question: Where does the trail start and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The route is a circular trail originating from Valdemoro. While the exact starting point isn't specified, Valdemoro serves as the general access point. Parking information is not detailed in the available data, but as a circular route from a town, local parking options in Valdemoro would be the most likely solution.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is quite varied, offering a diverse experience for touring cyclists. You'll encounter a mix of surfaces: approximately 17.3 km are unpaved paths, 12.5 km are asphalt, and 7.06 km are paved. Other sections include state roads, streets, and regular roads. Be prepared for some segments that are explicitly described as difficult to ride.

faqs.question: What are the main highlights and viewpoints along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers several notable points of interest. You'll pass by the impressive 15th-century Torrejón de Velasco Castle. Scenic viewpoints include Cerro Pelango, a great spot to take in the surroundings, and Cerro El Portillo, where you can enjoy the magnificent Valdemoro pine forest. You'll also cross the Guatén Stream Crossing, which might require navigating stones depending on water levels.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: While specific seasonal recommendations aren't provided, given the varied terrain and potential for unpaved sections, spring and autumn would generally offer the most pleasant cycling conditions with milder temperatures and potentially less dust or mud. Summer might be very hot, and winter could bring colder, wetter conditions.

faqs.question: Is the route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: The available information does not explicitly state whether the route is dog-friendly or specific leash rules. However, the 'Camino Hondo' section is a popular trail shared with walkers and runners, suggesting a general outdoor environment. It's always best to assume dogs should be kept on a leash and to follow local regulations regarding pets on trails.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: There is no mention of any permits or entrance fees required to access or cycle this route. It appears to be freely accessible for public use.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several other trails. You will cycle along parts of the Ruta Verde 2.2 Canal del Jarama, Camino de las Pilillas, Camino de Valdemoro, and the Cañada Real Galiana, among others. These intersections add to the route's diversity and connectivity within the local trail network.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink near the route?
faqs.answer: The route starts from Valdemoro, a town that would likely offer various cafes, restaurants, and pubs for refreshments before or after your ride. However, there are no specific establishments mentioned directly along the trail itself, so it's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks.

faqs.question: What should I pack for a touring bicycle ride on this trail?
faqs.answer: Given the route's moderate difficulty and varied terrain, you should pack essentials like sufficient water, snacks, a basic bike repair kit (including a spare tube and pump), and appropriate cycling attire. Sun protection, a helmet, and navigation tools are also highly recommended. Be prepared for potential changes in weather, especially if cycling during shoulder seasons.
